explanation: The natural language text requests that whenever an off-normal condition (interpreted as a failureDetected event) occurs, the system must always transition to a safe state (failSafe) afterwards. To guarantee safety after a detected failure at all times, this can be captured as: 'Globally, if a failure is detected, then from that point onwards, it is always the case that the system is in a safe state.' In LTL, this is formalized as G(failureDetected -> G(failSafe)). This ensures that for any point where failureDetected becomes true, failSafe will be true globally from that point forward. Previous properties that might only use eventual, like G(failureDetected -> F(failSafe)), do not guarantee that the system remains in the safe state after failure detection, which is required by the user's request for an absolute guarantee to always fail safe.

The input requirement is that if a failure (off-normal condition) is detected, the system must always guarantee that it enters a fail-safe state immediately (no delay or uncertainty). To capture this, we use the LTL formula G(failureDetected -> X failSafe), which states: globally (always), if a failure is detected, then in the next instant, the system must be in a fail-safe state. This captures the idea of 'immediately' (by using the next operator X), and 'always' (by using G). The specification guarantees that after any detected failure, the fail-safe mechanism is activated in the very next timestep, satisfying the request for an absolute guarantee of fail-safe response. This is more precise than weaker formulas such as G(failureDetected -> F failSafe), which would only require the system to eventually go fail-safe (but not immediately), and does not specify an immediate guarantee. Thus, G(failureDetected -> X failSafe) best captures the intent in the natural language input.

The requirement is to 'absolutely guarantee that it always fails safe' whenever an off normal condition (unexpected event) occurs. With the given atomic propositions, the most appropriate way to model 'always fails safe' in LTL is to require that globally (at every point in time), whenever a failure is detected, the system must enter a safe state (failSafe). This is expressed in LTL as G(failureDetected -> failSafe), meaning 'It is always the case that if a failure is detected, then the system enters a safe state.' This captures the requirement that the system must always react to off-normal situations with a fail-safe response. The property is strong (guaranteeing every failure results in failSafe), which fits the natural language's desire for absolute guarantee. Other slight variants (e.g., using 'eventually' rather than an immediate implication) are not as strong and may not satisfy the 'absolutely guarantee' wording.

The statement requests an absolute guarantee that the system always fails safe whenever an off-normal (unexpected/failure) condition is detected. In the atomic propositions, 'failureDetected' represents detection of a failure (or unexpected event), and 'failSafe' represents the system entering a safe state. To guarantee that the system always fails safe upon failure detection, we use the LTL property: G (failureDetected -> failSafe). This means 'globally, whenever a failure is detected, the system must be in a safe state.' This captures the required guarantee that at any point in time, if an off-normal condition (failureDetected) occurs, the system absolutely must fail safe (failSafe). No other LTL property is more precise in expressing this safety obligation. The use of 'G (failureDetected -> failSafe)' ensures that there is never an instance where a failure is detected and the system is not safe, reflecting the stakeholder’s desire for an absolute guarantee.

The specification requests that whenever an off-normal (unexpected) condition or a failure is detected, the system must ALWAYS fail safe, immediately and without exception. LTL captures such safety guarantees by stipulating that globally, if a failure is detected, then it must be the case that failSafe holds at the same time. This ensures at all points in time (globally) that if a failure is detected, 'failSafe' is true in that state—thus immediately (no delay) and inevitably failing safe. We do not include 'explainOffNormal' since the requirement focuses solely on the guarantee of failing safe, not on explanation. Prior LTL properties that use 'eventually' (F), like G(failureDetected -> F failSafe), are too weak—they allow an unsafe window after detection. Therefore, G(failureDetected -> failSafe) is stricter and better matches the absolute guarantee required.

The requirement is that whenever an off-normal condition or unexpected event (which can be identified through the occurrence of 'failureDetected') happens, the system must always fail safe—that is, it must respond by entering a safe state (failSafe) immediately or at least with no delay. In LTL, this is typically specified as 'Globally, if failure is detected, then immediately in the next state, the system ensures fail-safe'. The LTL property G(failureDetected -> X(failSafe)) expresses that globally, whenever a failure is detected, at the next moment, the system must be in a safe state. This matches the intent of always guaranteeing a fail-safe response, capturing the requirement that the system transitions to a safe state upon detection of an off-normal condition.

The requirement specifies two things: (1) Immediate awareness of an off normal condition (which translates to detecting a failure instantly when it occurs) and (2) a guarantee that the system always fails safe (i.e., whenever a failure is detected, the system responds by entering a safe state). In LTL, to guarantee that the system always fails safe, we express that globally, whenever failureDetected holds, failSafe must also hold (i.e., G(failureDetected -> failSafe)). This property ensures that in all reachable states, if a failure is detected, the system must be in a safe state. The clause 'immediately know' is not perfectly representable in standard LTL semantics, but by placing this within an implication (anywhere failureDetected becomes true, failSafe must hold at the same time), this is the closest expressible guarantee. The previously given properties, if any, that might only require failSafe to eventually follow failureDetected or not always upon detection, would allow unsafe windows; this strict implication prevents that.

The requirement specifies that when an off-normal condition (e.g., 'failureDetected') occurs, it must always be guaranteed that the system enters a safe state ('failSafe'). This suggests a safety property: globally, when a failure is detected, it must be accompanied (either immediately or subsequently) by entering a safe state, and no failure occurs without this guarantee. The LTL formula G(failureDetected -> F failSafe) expresses this: globally, whenever a failure is detected, it is eventually (possibly immediately) followed by the system entering a safe state. This correctly captures the 'always fails safe' requirement. Previous (unseen) LTLs may not have captured the guarantee (using G), or could have only required that sometimes failures are followed by safety, but not always.

The requirement asks that if an off-normal (unexpected/failure) condition occurs, the system must always fail safely. This translates to: whenever a failure is detected (i.e., 'failureDetected' becomes true), it must be guaranteed that the system transitions to or is in a safe state ('failSafe'). In LTL, this is typically captured by the property: globally, whenever 'failureDetected' occurs, it is always immediately followed (either instantly or eventually) by 'failSafe'. The most strict and direct guarantee is to require that it is always the case that if a failure is detected, then globally the system is or will be in a fail-safe state. This can be represented as G(failureDetected -> G(failSafe)), meaning that once failureDetected is true, the predicate failSafe holds at that point and all future points. This captures the 'absolutely guarantee that it always fails safe' sentiment.

The input natural language requests that, in the presence of an off-normal condition (i.e., something unexpected or a failure), the system must always guarantee a fail-safe response. Interpreting 'always fails safe' as 'whenever a failure is detected, the system will always subsequently be in a fail-safe state,' we use the LTL formula: G(failureDetected -> G(failSafe)). This formula asserts that globally, whenever a failure is detected, it is guaranteed that from that point onwards the system is always in a fail-safe state. This matches the requirement for an absolute guarantee rather than a one-off response. If a previous LTL property had only specified 'eventually' (e.g., G(failureDetected -> F(failSafe))), that would not have been strong enough—eventual fail-safe would allow non-safe operation right after detection; but the current property ensures persistent safety.
